comment: Detector logic lives in js/acl-engine.js — DETECTORS map. This file is the human-facing catalog used to render the mistake feed and the cheat-sheet sidecar.

traps.title: Order trap — permit any before deny
traps.summary: `permit ip any any` placed above a `deny ...` makes the deny line dead. ACLs are top-down, first-match-wins.
traps.fix: Move the deny ABOVE the permit-any.
traps.examQuote: Day 34: "Order matters — permit any before a deny X makes the deny useless."

traps.title: Forgotten implicit deny
traps.summary: Every ACL has an invisible `deny ip any any` at the end. If your ACL only has deny lines, everything else gets dropped.
traps.fix: Either add an explicit `permit ip any any` at the bottom, or trust the implicit deny on purpose.
traps.examQuote: Day 34: "Implicit deny is NOT visible in `show access-lists` — but it is always there."

traps.title: Wildcard inverted
traps.summary: Wildcard masks are the INVERSE of subnet masks. /24 = `0.0.0.255`, NOT `255.255.255.0`.
traps.examQuote: Day 34: "Wildcard mask is NOT subnet mask — it is the inverse."

traps.title: Bare IP in extended
traps.summary: Extended ACLs reject a bare IP. Must use `host X` or explicit wildcard `0.0.0.0`.
traps.examQuote: Day 35: "Bare IP without `host` is NOT valid in extended ACLs."

traps.title: Standard ACL placed near source
traps.summary: Standard ACLs filter on source IP only — placing one near source over-blocks every dest, not just the one you wanted to protect.
traps.fix: Apply Standard ACL CLOSE TO DESTINATION.
traps.examQuote: Day 34: "Place standard ACLs near the destination — else you over-block."

traps.title: Extended ACL placed near destination
traps.summary: Extended ACLs are specific. Placing them near the destination wastes upstream bandwidth carrying traffic that will be dropped anyway.
traps.fix: Apply Extended ACL CLOSE TO SOURCE.
traps.examQuote: Day 35: "Apply close to source — drop unwanted traffic before it travels."

traps.title: Wrong direction (in vs out)
traps.summary: An ACL applied `in` checks packets ENTERING the interface; `out` checks LEAVING. Wrong direction = packet never inspected.
traps.fix: Trace the packet's path: which interface does it enter / leave on the router that owns this ACL?
traps.examQuote: Day 34: "One ACL per interface per direction."

traps.title: Two ACLs same interface, same direction
traps.summary: Cisco only allows ONE ACL per interface per direction. A second `ip access-group` in the same direction REPLACES the first.
traps.fix: Combine the rules into a single ACL.
traps.examQuote: Day 34: "You CANNOT apply two ACLs in the same direction on one interface."

traps.title: HTTP/HTTPS over UDP
traps.summary: HTTP=80 and HTTPS=443 are TCP. Common quiz trap.

traps.title: All-deny ACL — implicit deny eats unrelated traffic
traps.summary: Only deny lines means the implicit `deny any` drops every other packet too — even traffic you wanted to permit.
traps.fix: Add `permit ip any any` (extended) or `permit any` (standard) as the final entry.
traps.examQuote: Day 35: "Implicit deny ip any any at end — always remember explicit permit if needed."

traps.title: host + wildcard mutually exclusive
traps.summary: `host 1.1.1.1 0.0.0.255` is invalid. `host` is its own /32 shortcut.
traps.examQuote: Day 34: "host = /32 shortcut; adding wildcard is invalid."

traps.title: VTY uses `access-class`, not `access-group`
traps.summary: On `line vty 0 4` the command to bind an ACL is `access-class N in`. `ip access-group` is for interfaces.
traps.fix: Switch to `access-class N in` under `line vty`.
traps.examQuote: Day 35 EXTRA: "VTY ACL applies inbound with `access-class N in`."

traps.title: Numbered ACL refuses single-line insert
traps.summary: In numbered global config you cannot insert or delete a single entry. Convert to NAMED ACL — then sequence numbers let you insert / delete cleanly.
traps.fix: `ip access-list extended NAME` then `15 permit ...` to insert.
traps.examQuote: Day 35: "Named mode: can insert / delete single entries."

traps.title: `established` on UDP
traps.summary: `established` matches TCP packets with ACK or RST set. UDP has no connection state — flag is invalid.
traps.examQuote: Day 35 EXTRA: "established matches return TCP traffic with ACK/RST."

traps.title: ACL number range mismatched to ACL type
traps.fix: Pick a number that matches the rule type, OR convert to a NAMED ACL.
traps.examQuote: Day 34: standard ranges. Day 35: extended ranges.

traps.title: Forgot the `log` keyword for an audit
traps.summary: Without `log`, you can see hit counters but no per-match syslog event. Use `log` when you need an audit trail.
traps.fix: Append `log` to the ACE you want to audit.
traps.examQuote: Day 34 EXTRA: `log` triggers syslog on every match.
